speaker: interviewer
text: It's January the 8th, 1995. The survivor is Sam Koslowski. Jody Kleinman, interviewer. It's in Beverly Hills, California, USA. And it will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have the privilege today, January 8, 1996, of interviewing Sam Koslowski. My name is Jody Kleinman, we're in Beverly Hills, California, and the language we'll be speaking is English. Good afternoon. Can you please tell me your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam, Samuel, Koslowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your last name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-S-L-O-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Wisznice, 18 May, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 75, Close to 76.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Chaim Koslowksi. My mother was-- her name was Craindle Koslowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did have. I had four brothers and five sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blacksmith.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you give me the names of your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my father's parents. The mother was Esther, and the father was Nachum.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have any other members of your extended family? Cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do have about 40, 50 of them, cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe your Jewish background for me when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to yeshiva all the time. And after yeshiva lesson, I was going to the Polish school through the seventh grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember celebrating Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe one in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had Pesach, we had Shavuot. We had Rosh Hashana. We have Yom Kippur. We had Sukkot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you celebrate it with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did celebrate with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me your schooling? I need more detail about your schooling. Describe the schools, what you did during a day.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go to school, to the Polish school. They were big anti-Semites in the school. You know, the teacher tried to keep them down, not to discriminate so much. Like the kids in the school discriminate against Jewish people. And we went to school until seventh grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what ways did you feel the anti-Semitism from the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The anti-Semitism was so big. For example, the Polish children, they used to call me all the time, Jew, Jew. And I didn't pay attention. Well, one time they started to fight with me, there was a-- and I ran to the teacher, and I told the teacher, they-- they don't let me pass by the street. And the teacher quieted them down-- stopped them, and not to discriminate so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your house for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was not far from the school. It was a brick house. We had four rooms, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oil [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in this-- in this house. We used to have a barn which we used to flour the-- the-- the-- flour the-- the-- how do you call? The jars? Make it flour. Later, they used to bring it into the house, and my mother used to give them such a big dish, and they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made it softer, the flour, and they-- they made it oil. And my father used to be a blacksmith, and he used to have extra-- a bungalow for the blacksmith. All my brothers were blacksmiths, because my father helped them to do this. And I didn't like it. I, myself-- my brother-in-law had a flour mill, and I used to run away to the flour mill to help my brother-in-law by the mortars-- which he likes it. And he-- I did a lot of work for him, which he used to give me some food [LAUGHS] for the job. And I was very happy, till my father saw I am not there. He used to beat me up. I should come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of your friends from that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a lot of friends. We used to go to school and we used to go to yeshiva, we used to go. And we used to play together, and we had a lot of fun. I liked it. It was very nice. There was no discrimination between friends. It was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any specific memories of either your mother or your father that you'd like to share with us today?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean 'to share'?

speaker: interviewer
text: To talk--to tell us about anything in particular about your mother or father that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do remember my father. I'm sorry-- I have to mention like I used to run away to the flour mill, and he used to catch me and he used to beat me up. And that I should go back to be a blacksmith. And I never like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of your friends? Any specific things that you'd like to tell us about any particular friend or event that happened in your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember a special friends. I just remember we used to go to school together. We had a lot of fun. It was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have to make any changes in your daily life because of the anti-Semitism that you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you explain to me what you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there was a lot of anti-Semitism at the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to change your behavior at all because of the anti-Semitism? Did your parents give you any advice about that or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- [CRYING] my mother was so nice. She used to tell me, ignore them. Run by them and just go tell the teacher they bother you, which I did. And one time, it was a Saturday morning, I and my friend watched the boys, which they used to do a lot of harm to me, we used to watch them. And when they run out for school, we used to beat them up. Not special beat them up, but we tried to, for example, to make believe we're going to beat them up. And the galach came across. He was running on-- on the wagon--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that name that you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: The galach. A galach. You know what it means? A galach?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Can you translate that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the church where they used to sit-- he used to pass by, on a wagon, he was sitting, and he stopped it and I told him the boys, they were beating me up and they discriminated me. And he took them away and stopped them. That he was from the church, that one. The priest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your first signs that danger was coming? Did you have any warnings before 1939 that there were problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, The war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And before that, were there any signs of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, there was danger because they used to beat me up, all the boys from the school, which they belonged to a group, a church group, and they used to beat me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big was your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about, for example, there was about 2,000, 3,000 Jews were living. And about 2,000, 3,000 Catholics were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you explain the Jewish community to me there? Did everyone worship together? How was the Jewish community organized?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The Jewish community didn't-- together. They never worshipped together. They-- in our synagogue, we used to go to synagogue Saturday in the morning and at night, every day. And we didn't bother with the Catholics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Jewish community, itself, close to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. They were close to each other. Yeah. They were nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then can you tell me what happened in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, I was a very wild boy, full of life. I was full of life. For example, they said-- the German came to our town in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] vehicles-- what do you call this? I forgot the name, the big tanks-- tanks. They came over and I was on the spot in the-- in the-- in the market. There was a market there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he called me, and maybe recognizing me, I'm Jewish, or maybe not. And he asked me, do you have-- does a lot of Jews live here? I said, not too much, because a lot of them went to Russian-- to the Russian zone, when the Russians came close by and the Germans came out of sight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, I feel sorry for you-- he didn't say for me-- I feel sorry for the Jews, because we're going to kill them. And I think it's funny. And he run away-- run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, for example, 1939-- they came in September, October. The Germans came. We didn't bother with them too much. The Polish people, they used to attack Jews in the city. We used to call the Germans, they should come, because to avoid the Polish people should attack the Jews. They came, they didn't bother too much. The Germans didn't bother too much. In 19-- in May, 1940, the Germans started to catch Jewish boys and girls and to take them to the concentration camps. I was the one which they called me. They took me not far from the city, about 20 miles away, in a small city they called Rozwadowka. They took me there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In this-- they made empty the school, which the kids who used to go to-- they didn't go to school-- and they put us in-- in the school, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few days later, they took us to work. They took us to work in a-- in a lawn, in a lawn in which grass had been grown. And they gave us shovels, and they started to tell us to dig in the middle rivers-- and-- to make a river. The water should go inside in the middle of the lawn, not all over the lawn. I worked over there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was May, June, July, August, September. I became sick. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There came a doctor-- a Polish Jew-- a doctor. He told me I have pneumonia. [CRYING] He took me to [NON-ENGLISH]. He took me to a hospital, with several more boys. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I was there about two, three weeks, and I became better. I remember we didn't have what to eat. There was a family, our family, that lived-- [CRYING] and she brought me some food. I ate the food she brought me every day, the food, till the doctor came and said, you have to go home. All right. It became better, the pneumonia-- the pneumonia. And I went home. I went home. 2 December, a Polish man came to me, a very nice man, he said to me, come to me to work in the flour mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we go back for a minute and describe the living conditions for the months that you were digging the ravines. What were your living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- for example, they gave us shovels. They gave us-- we used to go every morning to the place where we used to dig the ground to make the rivers. Oh, by the way, the-- the-- the engineer from the-- which he took us, his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember very well his name, because he became friends with me. He used to make comments to me and joked around with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other people were there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were about 500 people there. Mostly they were from Miedzyrzec, from a big city. They were about 30,000, 40,000 Jews living there. They brought them over from Miedzyrzec to the-- to the Rozwadowka. And we used to go to work all of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept in the-- on the floor with the school. On the floor. The flies and the lice used to eat us up. You know, we had straw on the floor, and just a blanket. My sister brought it to me and gave it to me. I was there, and my brother was there. An older brother, like me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My sister-- we had a family, they used to live in the city. My sister, Chana Sarah, she used to cook for us. She had her husband there, and we had Moshe, my brother, and I was there. She used to cook for us. She couldn't go inside in the ghetto. No, she brought the food outside the wires, and I used to-- and we used to take the food to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that time was there any discussion amongst the workers about what was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Nothing then. We never knew they was going to come a time in which they were going to kill us-- want to kill us. We never talked about, because the rest of the people from Miedzyrzec, when it got cold in January, they sent them home, too. They went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you hear any news of what was going on in the rest of the countries nearby? Did you hear any outside news?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't-- you know, you-- you can't go to the other-- to the other cities to hear news, because the Germans would kill you on the road. We didn't hear nothing from the other cities besides our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you mentioned that you were about to tell me about going to work at the flour mill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me that, what happened next after the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: In December, I used to-- I started to go to work. Three Germans came and caught me, and I said to him, what do you want from me? I'm not a Jew. Let me go. They said to me, oh, no. I don't believe you. Let's go to the lady-- her name was Gerchinska-- Ger-chin-ska. He took me in to the lady-- they had a store. They had German things for sale for the Polish people. They took me in, and they asked Gerchinska-- first of all, I talk before she talked. I said, I don't know what they want. I'm not a Jew. And they said-- it was three Germans-- they said to her, tell me if he's a Jew or not. She said-- she knows me very well-- she says, I don't know him. And they let me go. They let me go to work. I told the [NON-ENGLISH], my boss, the Germans want to catch me, they want to take me away, and later they would kill me there. He went over to the German [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH], they called him. He was a-- a big [NON-ENGLISH], a big man. And he brought me a paper, which nobody should touch me. And I used to go to work. I had a good time. I liked it because, you know, he's the one-- he was such a nice man-- he's the one who wanted to help Jews. He used to give me flour, he used to give me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] cereals I should take them home, and we should-- and I should give it to the Jewish people, because they-- we didn't live in the ghetto, our family didn't live in the ghetto. Well, we used to live three or four sisters in one house. And they brought over, in the ghetto, in Mlawa, people, they should live in the ghetto. I used to go in once in a while in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people didn't have straw to sleep in, straw. They used to sleep on a board, [NON-ENGLISH]. You don't understand? On a board. Like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to saw little kids used to die from hunger. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They couldn't walk, they couldn't move. And the mothers used to go to us-- we weren't in the ghetto-- to steal bread, to steal potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's stop right now, and then we'll continue.
